museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: November lets the Amiga show off with Defender of the Crown, while Sega's home identity grows.
museum.curatorNote: November has strong computer and Sega context, with some month-level caveats.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments, with broad context separated from confident month-level claims.

items.title: Defender of the Crown showcases the Amiga
items.body: Cinemaware's strategy-adventure hybrid sells the machine as a cinematic games platform.
items.sourceNote: 1986 is secure; month varies across sources.

items.title: Alex Kidd in Miracle World arrives for Sega's Mark III/Master System line
items.body: Sega's platform hero gives the system a mascot-like identity before Sonic.
items.sourceNote: November 1986 is widely cited for Japan.

items.title: The Amiga starts to look like a games machine
items.body: Its graphics and sound can now be sold through software, not just specifications.
items.sourceNote: Defender of the Crown context.

items.title: UK players see aspiration and reality side by side
items.body: Magazine screenshots of Amiga games sit beside affordable Spectrum and C64 tapes.
items.sourceNote: UK hardware context.

items.title: Christmas format arguments sharpen
items.body: Spectrum +2, CPC, C64, Atari ST and Amiga all occupy different dreams and budgets.
items.sourceNote: UK retail context.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable games from the year, led by month-specific anchors where the evidence supports them.

items.title: Defender of the Crown
items.body: Cinemaware sells the Amiga as a machine for games that look like moving illustrations.
items.sourceNote: 1986 is secure; month varies in sources.

items.title: The Legend of Zelda
items.body: Nintendo turns exploration, secrets and item discovery into a new console ritual.
items.sourceNote: Japanese FDS release date is widely cited as 21 February 1986.

items.title: Fantasy Zone
items.body: Sega's pastel shooter makes arcade space feel bright, strange and toy-like.
items.sourceNote: March 1986 is commonly cited.

items.title: Wonder Boy
items.body: Sega and Westone's running platformer adds momentum, fruit and skateboard charm.
items.sourceNote: April 1986 is cited in several summaries.

items.title: Dragon Quest
items.body: Enix's console RPG becomes a Japanese phenomenon and helps define Famicom role-playing.
items.sourceNote: Japanese release date is widely cited as 27 May 1986.

items.title: Bubble Bobble
items.body: Taito's bubble-trapping co-op game becomes one of the year's most enduring arcade designs.
items.sourceNote: 1986 is secure; exact month varies by region/source.

items.title: Arkanoid
items.body: Taito rebuilds Breakout for the power-up age.
items.sourceNote: July 1986 is common in arcade summaries.

items.title: Metroid
items.body: Exploration, isolation and backtracking make Nintendo's sci-fi world feel unusually lonely.
items.sourceNote: Japanese FDS date is widely cited as 6 August 1986.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four machines or technology contexts that explain the month's place in gaming history.

items.title: Famicom Disk System
items.body: Nintendo's disk add-on hosts Zelda, Metroid and Kid Icarus in its first year.
items.sourceNote: Launched 21 February 1986.

items.title: ZX Spectrum +2
items.body: Amstrad's cassette-deck Spectrum changes the look of Sinclair gaming in British shops.
items.sourceNote: Used for late-1986 UK retail context.

items.title: Sega Master System / Mark III
items.body: Sega's home-console line grows through 1986, even if its UK impact blooms later.
items.sourceNote: Release timing varies by region.

items.title: Amiga and Atari ST
items.body: 16-bit home computers are becoming a dream tier above most children's tape machines.
items.sourceNote: Used for UK aspiration context.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Before online gaming was ordinary, paper, shops and local conversations carried the culture.

items.title: Compunet and Micronet hint at a future
items.body: A small slice of UK users can see networked services, but most players still learn through paper and people.

items.title: Letters pages are slow communities
items.body: Arguments about machines, scores and broken games appear weeks after they are posted.

items.title: BBS culture is specialist
items.body: Modems exist, but they are not the normal doorway into games for British children.

items.title: The schoolyard remains the feed
items.body: Rumours about Zelda, imports, arcade conversions and Spectrum secrets travel by voice.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading of the month as a player might have met it.

items.title: The world was splitting into timelines
items.body: November 1986 could hold Zelda in Japan, NES momentum in America and cassette games in a British bedroom.

items.title: Screenshots sold dreams
items.body: Magazine pages made Amiga, arcade and Japanese console games feel close even when they were not.

items.title: The microcomputer argument continued
items.body: Spectrum +2, CPC, C64, BBC, ST and Amiga all carried different budgets and identities.

items.title: Arcades felt like holidays
items.body: Out Run and Bubble Bobble belonged to bright public spaces before they became home-conversion expectations.

editorNotes: Initial AI-generated research draft for November 1986.
Month-specific dates are used only where the reviewed sources support them; uncertain items are marked in sourceNote/certainty fields.
Magazine cover images are reconstructed placeholders and should be replaced with verified/licensed scans if used editorially.
